content.full_text:
September 24, 1991




Lorraine Loo
Personnel Analyst
State Of California
395 Oyster Point Blvd.
So. San Francisco, CA  94080

                                        Re:  Your Request For Advice
                                             Our File No. A-91-364

Dear Ms. Loo:

     You have requested confirmation of telephone advice on behalf
of Workers' Compensation Judge Zimmerman concerning the
application of the honoraria and gift provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

                                    QUESTION

     May Judge Zimmerman accept transportation to and
accommodations in Hawaii to make presentations at a workers'
compensation seminar?

                                    CONCLUSION

     Transportation to and accommodations in Hawaii given to Judge
Zimmerman to make presentations at a workers' compensation seminar
are not subject to the gift and honoraria limits of the Act.
However, depending on the facts, such items may be reportable and
cause for a conflict of interest.

                                    FACTS

     Judge Zimmerman has been invited to make presentations at a
workers' compensation seminar set in Hawaii.  The sponsor of the

___________________________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  ●  P.O. Box 807  ●  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  ●  (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-91-364
Page 2


seminar has offered to pay Judge Zimmerman's transportation to and
accommodations in Hawaii. The sponsor of the seminar is a private
doctor.

ANALYSIS

     Effective January 1, 1991 certain provisions of the Act
regarding gifts and honoraria were amended. The Act now provides
that:

          No member of a state board or commission, and
     no designated employee of a state agency, shall
     accept an honorarium from any source, if the member
     or employee would be required to report the receipt
     of income or gifts from that source on his or her
     statement of economic interest. This section shall
     not apply to any part-time member of the governing
     board of any public institution of higher
     education, unless the member is also an elected
     official.

                              Section 89503(a).

          No member of a state board or commission, and
     no designated employee of a state agency, shall
     accept gifts with a total value of more than two
     hundred fifty dollars ($250) in a calendar year
     from any single source, if the member or employee
     would be required to report the receipt of income
     or gifts from that source on his or her statement
     of economic interest. This paragraph shall not
     apply to any part-time member of the governing
     board of any public institution of higher
     education, unless the member is also an elected
     official.

                              Section 89504(a).

     Judge Zimmerman is a designated employee of a state agency.

     The Commission will be considering the adoption of
regulations in the near future to clarify and implement the new
statutes. In the meantime, the Commission does have existing
regulations regarding limitations on gifts and travel. Although
these regulations predate the current statutes cited above, staff
continues to provide interim advice in accordance with these
regulations. Current Regulation 18728 provides as follows:

          [F]ree admission, food, beverages and similar
     nominal benefits provided to a filer at an event at
     which he or she speaks, participates in a panel or
     seminar or performs a similar service, and
     reimbursement or advance for actual intrastate
     travel and for necessary accommodations provided

Our File No. A-91-364
Page 3


     directly in connection with the event are not
     payments and need not be reported by the filer.

                           Regulation 18728(a), emphasis
                           added.

     We have previously advised that the term "accommodations" includes
lodging and meals.  (See Puglia Advice Letter, No. A-77-464; Chel
Advice Letter, No. A-78-264; Tebbetts Advice Letter, No. A-
186.)  The extent to which lodging and meals are "necessary" in
connection with speaking at an event must be considered on a case-
by-case basis.  At a minimum, lodging the night before or the
night after a speaking event when out-of-town travel is necessary
generally would meet this standard.  Whether a second night's
lodging also is necessary would depend on circumstances such as
the time and length of event.  For example, an event which is
scheduled only in the morning or afternoon ordinarily would
require only one night's lodging.  In contrast, an all-day event
could require lodging the night before and the night of the event.
Similar reasoning would apply to determine whether meals are
"necessary."  Please note, however, that any accommodations
provided to a state official which are not necessary or directly
in connection with the official's speaking engagement may be gifts
and not covered by this very limited exception.

     Transportation to Hawaii, as it is interstate, does not fall
within the exception of Regulation 18728 and is reported, may be
limited, and can be cause for a conflict of interest.

     Whether the transportation and accommodation not exempted by
Regulation 18728 are limited depends on analysis under Section
89506.  Under Section 89506(a), transportation and related lodging
and subsistence which are directly in connection with a speaking
event are not subject to limits as follows:

     (a)  Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, including actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:

          (1)  The travel is in connection with a
speech given by the elected state officer,
member of a state board or commission, or
designated employee of a state agency, the
lodging and subsistence expenses are limited

Our File No. A-91-364
Page 4


to the day immediately preceding, the day of,
and the day immediately following the speech,
and the travel is within the United States.

* * *

Section 89506(a).

Thus, while payments for transportation to Hawaii are reportable,
such payments are not limited if they fall under Section
89506(a)(1).2  Also, please note that although the gift of
transportation may not be limited, if the judge receives gifts,
including transportation, aggregating $250 of more in value prior
to a decision affecting the donor, the judge may have a conflict
of interest.3

If you have any further questions regarding this matter
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.4

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel


By: Mark T. Morodomi
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MM:aa


_______________________

2  Gifts valued at $50 or more must be disclosed.  (Sections
87207, 87302.)

3  You indicated in a telephone conversation with me on August 27,
1991, that your request for advice does not request advice on any
conflict of interest issues.  Should the judge wish to avoid any
potential concerns of disclosure or conflict of interest, he may
reimburse the donor of the gift for all or a portion of the value
of the gift.  If he does so, the value of the gift is reduced by
the amount of reimbursement.  (Regulation 18726.1.)

4  Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.analysis:
Effective January 1, 1991 certain provisions of the Act regarding gifts and honoraria were amended. The Act now provides that:

No member of a state board or commission, and no designated employee of a state agency, shall accept an honorarium from any source, if the member or employee would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic interest. This section shall not apply to any part-time member of the governing board of any public institution of higher education, unless the member is also an elected official.

Section 89503(a).

No member of a state board or commission, and no designated employee of a state agency, shall accept gifts with a total value of more than two hundred fifty dollars ($250) in a calendar year from any single source, if the member or employee would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic interest. This paragraph shall not apply to any part-time member of the governing board of any public institution of higher education, unless the member is also an elected official.

Section 89504(a).

Judge Zimmerman is a designated employee of a state agency.

The Commission will be considering the adoption of regulations in the near future to clarify and implement the new statutes. In the meantime, the Commission does have existing regulations regarding limitations on gifts and travel. Although these regulations predate the current statutes cited above, staff continues to provide interim advice in accordance with these regulations. Current Regulation 18728 provides as follows:

[F]ree admission, food, beverages and similar nominal benefits provided to a filer at an event at which he or she speaks, participates in a panel or seminar or performs a similar service, and reimbursement or advance for actual intrastate travel and for necessary accommodations provided

directly in connection with the event are not payments and need not be reported by the filer.

Regulation 18728(a), emphasis added.

We have previously advised that the term "accommodations" includes lodging and meals. (See Puglia Advice Letter, No. A-77-464; Chel Advice Letter, No. A-78-264; Tebbetts Advice Letter, No. I-91-186.) The extent to which lodging and meals are "necessary" in connection with speaking at an event must be considered on a case-by-case basis. At a minimum, lodging the night before or the night after a speaking event when out-of-town travel is necessary generally would meet this standard. Whether a second night's lodging also is necessary would depend on circumstances such as the time and length of the event. For example, an event which is scheduled only in the morning or afternoon ordinarily would require only one night's lodging. In contrast, an all-day event could require lodging the night before and the night of the event. Similar reasoning would apply to determine whether meals are "necessary." Please note, however, that any accommodations provided to a state official which are not necessary or directly in connection with the official's speaking engagement may be gifts and not covered by this very limited exception.

Transportation to Hawaii, as it is interstate, does not fall within the exception of Regulation 18728 and is reported, may be limited, and can be cause for a conflict of interest.

Whether the transportation and accommodation not exempted by Regulation 18728 are limited depends on analysis under Section 89506. Under Section 89506(a), transportation and related lodging and subsistence which are directly in connection with a speaking event are not subject to limits as follows:

(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or international public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of the following apply:

(1) The travel is in connection with a speech given by the elected state officer, member of a state board or commission, or designated employee of a state agency, the lodging and subsistence expenses are limited

to the day immediately preceding, the day of, and the day immediately following the speech, and the travel is within the United States.

However, depending on the facts, such items may be reportable and cause for a conflict of interest.
